item.title: SeqPE: Transformer with Sequential Position Encoding
item.dc:creator: Huayang Li
Yahui Liu
Hongyu Sun
Deng Cai
Leyang Cui
Wei Bi
Peilin Zhao
Taro Watanabe
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: Since self-attention layers in Transformers are permutation invariant by design, positional encodings must be explicitly incorporated to enable spatial understanding. However, fixed-size lookup tables used in traditional learnable position embeddings (PEs) limit extrapolation capabilities beyond pre-trained sequence lengths. Expert-designed methods such as ALIBI and ROPE, mitigate this limitation but demand extensive modifications for adapting to new modalities, underscoring fundamental challenges in adaptability and scalability. In this work, we present SEQPE, a unified and fully learnable position encoding framework that represents each n-dimensional position index as a symbolic sequence and employs a lightweight sequential position encoder to learn their embeddings in an end-to-end manner. To regularize SEQPE's embedding space, we introduce two complementary objectives: a contrastive objective that aligns embedding distances with a predefined position-distance function, and a knowledge distillation loss that anchors out-of-distribution position embeddings to in-distribution teacher representations, further enhancing extrapolation performance. Experiments across language modeling, long-context question answering, and 2D image classification demonstrate that SeqPE not only surpasses strong baselines in perplexity, exact match (EM), and accuracy—particularly under context length extrapolation—but also enables seamless generalization to multi-dimensional inputs without requiring manual architectural redesign. Published: 2026-05-20T19:52:22+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.837 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Huayang Li; Yahui Liu; Hongyu Sun; Deng Cai; Leyang Cui; Wei Bi; Peilin Zhao; Taro Watanabe

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3695094

Relevance: 0.837 (must_read)

Since self-attention layers in Transformers are permutation invariant by design, positional encodings must be explicitly incorporated to enable spatial understanding. However, fixed-size lookup tables used in traditional learnable position embeddings (PEs) limit extrapolation capabilities beyond pre-trained sequence lengths. Expert-designed methods such as ALIBI and ROPE, mitigate this limitation but demand extensive modifications for adapting to new modalities, underscoring fundamental challenges in adaptability and scalability. In this work, we present SEQPE, a unified and fully learnable position encoding framework that represents each n-dimensional position index as a symbolic sequence and employs a lightweight sequential position encoder to learn their embeddings in an end-to-end manner. To regularize SEQPE's embedding space, we introduce two complementary objectives: a contrastive objective that aligns embedding distances with a predefined position-distance function, and a knowledge distillation loss that anchors out-of-distribution position embeddings to in-distribution teacher representations, further enhancing extrapolation performance. Experiments across language modeling, long-context question answering, and 2D image classification demonstrate that SeqPE not only surpasses strong baselines in perplexity, exact match (EM), and accuracy—particularly under context length extrapolation—but also enables seamless generalization to multi-dimensional inputs without requiring manual architectural redesign.

item.title: Partial Contrastive Learning for Partially View-aligned Multi-view Clustering
item.dc:creator: Gehui Xu
Chengliang Liu
Yabo Liu
Yong Xu
Jie Wen
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: Multi-view clustering has achieved promising performance with the advancement in deep neural networks. However, most existing multi-view clustering methods assume that cross-view correspondences are fully known, which is often unrealistic in real-world scenarios due to factors such as temporal asynchrony and sensor faults. To address this, we propose a novel Partial Contrastive Learning (PCL) framework that tackles the practical yet understudied problem of partially aligned multi-view clustering. The PCL combines explicit cross-view correspondence modeling with the contrastive learning paradigm. Specifically, PCL integrates a partial alignment module to mitigate inherent misalignment by adaptively computing soft matching probabilities. These probabilities, in turn, guide a generalized contrastive loss to learn robust representations from both aligned and unaligned data. Overall, our PCL effectively enhances the discriminative power of the learned multi-view representations, achieving superior alignment and clustering performance. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the PCL through extensive comparative experiments with eleven partially aligned multi-view clustering methods on eight benchmark datasets. Published: 2026-05-20T19:52:22+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.835 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Gehui Xu; Chengliang Liu; Yabo Liu; Yong Xu; Jie Wen

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3694999

Relevance: 0.835 (must_read)

Multi-view clustering has achieved promising performance with the advancement in deep neural networks. However, most existing multi-view clustering methods assume that cross-view correspondences are fully known, which is often unrealistic in real-world scenarios due to factors such as temporal asynchrony and sensor faults. To address this, we propose a novel Partial Contrastive Learning (PCL) framework that tackles the practical yet understudied problem of partially aligned multi-view clustering. The PCL combines explicit cross-view correspondence modeling with the contrastive learning paradigm. Specifically, PCL integrates a partial alignment module to mitigate inherent misalignment by adaptively computing soft matching probabilities. These probabilities, in turn, guide a generalized contrastive loss to learn robust representations from both aligned and unaligned data. Overall, our PCL effectively enhances the discriminative power of the learned multi-view representations, achieving superior alignment and clustering performance. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the PCL through extensive comparative experiments with eleven partially aligned multi-view clustering methods on eight benchmark datasets.

item.title: Self-training Based Source-free Domain Adaptation for Remote Sensing Scene Classification
item.dc:creator: Shuo Han
Xu Tang
Jingjing Ma
Xiangrong Zhang
Fang Liu
Licheng Jiao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Remote sensing scene classification (RSSC) is crucial for interpreting remote sensing (RS) images across various applications. Existing deep learning methods have promoted RSSC results to a new level. However, most of them rely on an assumption that training and test data are independently and identically distributed. While this requirement can be readily met under laboratory conditions, it proves to be exceptionally stringent in practical settings, as the collected new RS images may differ significantly in distribution from the RS images used to train the classification model. In this cross-domain case, many successful models would be disabled. To address the above challenges and enhance the practicability of the RSSC model, this paper proposes a self-training based source-free domain adaptation (SFDA) method named STSFDA. First, a multi-task source domain pre-training strategy is developed to provide a strong initialization for the SFDA process. Next, a teacher-student network is adopted to drive the SFDA process. In the teacher network, target RS scenes are divided into two groups based on confidence and then passed to the student network. The student network applies supervised learning to reliable samples and unsupervised learning to uncertain samples. A neighbor-guided pseudo-labeling strategy is also introduced to generate more reliable pseudo-labels with the consideration of neighborhood information. In addition, mixup learning and contrastive learning are applied to all target domain samples to obtain more discriminative feature representations. Experimental results demonstrate that STSFDA consistently outperforms many existing baseline models, highlighting its effectiveness and potential for future research in the RS community. Code is available at https://github.com/TangXu-Group/STSFDA. Published: 2026-05-21T19:44:36+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.834 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shuo Han; Xu Tang; Jingjing Ma; Xiangrong Zhang; Fang Liu; Licheng Jiao

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3695530

Relevance: 0.834 (must_read)

Remote sensing scene classification (RSSC) is crucial for interpreting remote sensing (RS) images across various applications. Existing deep learning methods have promoted RSSC results to a new level. However, most of them rely on an assumption that training and test data are independently and identically distributed. While this requirement can be readily met under laboratory conditions, it proves to be exceptionally stringent in practical settings, as the collected new RS images may differ significantly in distribution from the RS images used to train the classification model. In this cross-domain case, many successful models would be disabled. To address the above challenges and enhance the practicability of the RSSC model, this paper proposes a self-training based source-free domain adaptation (SFDA) method named STSFDA. First, a multi-task source domain pre-training strategy is developed to provide a strong initialization for the SFDA process. Next, a teacher-student network is adopted to drive the SFDA process. In the teacher network, target RS scenes are divided into two groups based on confidence and then passed to the student network. The student network applies supervised learning to reliable samples and unsupervised learning to uncertain samples. A neighbor-guided pseudo-labeling strategy is also introduced to generate more reliable pseudo-labels with the consideration of neighborhood information. In addition, mixup learning and contrastive learning are applied to all target domain samples to obtain more discriminative feature representations. Experimental results demonstrate that STSFDA consistently outperforms many existing baseline models, highlighting its effectiveness and potential for future research in the RS community. Code is available at https://github.com/TangXu-Group/STSFDA.

item.title: Robust detector-free multimodal image matching based on visual model guidance and gated attention
item.dc:creator: Tengfeng Tang
Zhiqiang Han
Tao Peng
Jinhao Chen
Yuanxin Ye
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Multimodal image matching is a crucial foundational task for multi-source information fusion. However, severe geometric distortions and Non-linear Radiometric Differences (NRD) pose significant challenges to robust matching, such as the poor repeatability of keypoints. While detector-free methods emerge to mitigate this, current approaches relying on global context capture inadvertently aggregate irrelevant background noise, leading to reduced feature discriminability. To address these issues, this paper proposes a multimodal image matching method based on Visual Model Guidance and Gated Attention. First, we design a dual-stream backbone network that performs multi-scale fusion of high-level semantic priors from a visual foundation model with local geometric features from standard convolutions. This effectively enhances feature robustness against NRD and guides the model to focus on content-consistent regions. Second, to resolve the feature over-smoothing caused by global interactions, we propose a Gated Linear Attention (GLA) module. By employing a dynamic gating mechanism to filter out irrelevant global background noise, this module maximizes the preservation of local feature discriminability while incorporating effective long-range dependencies. Finally, we construct a coarse-to-fine multi-stage matching pipeline that cascades robust coarse matching with precise pixel-level refinement, achieving high-precision correspondence. Extensive experiments on four multimodal datasets (Optical-Infrared, Optical-SAR, Optical-Map, and Optical-Depth) demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming existing detector-based and detector-free baselines in terms of accuracy and robustness. The implementation of the proposed method will be made publicly available at https://github.com/yeyuanxin110/VMGGA . Published: 2026-05-21T17:23:17+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.824 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tengfeng Tang; Zhiqiang Han; Tao Peng; Jinhao Chen; Yuanxin Ye

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.05.005

Relevance: 0.824 (must_read)

Multimodal image matching is a crucial foundational task for multi-source information fusion. However, severe geometric distortions and Non-linear Radiometric Differences (NRD) pose significant challenges to robust matching, such as the poor repeatability of keypoints. While detector-free methods emerge to mitigate this, current approaches relying on global context capture inadvertently aggregate irrelevant background noise, leading to reduced feature discriminability. To address these issues, this paper proposes a multimodal image matching method based on Visual Model Guidance and Gated Attention. First, we design a dual-stream backbone network that performs multi-scale fusion of high-level semantic priors from a visual foundation model with local geometric features from standard convolutions. This effectively enhances feature robustness against NRD and guides the model to focus on content-consistent regions. Second, to resolve the feature over-smoothing caused by global interactions, we propose a Gated Linear Attention (GLA) module. By employing a dynamic gating mechanism to filter out irrelevant global background noise, this module maximizes the preservation of local feature discriminability while incorporating effective long-range dependencies. Finally, we construct a coarse-to-fine multi-stage matching pipeline that cascades robust coarse matching with precise pixel-level refinement, achieving high-precision correspondence. Extensive experiments on four multimodal datasets (Optical-Infrared, Optical-SAR, Optical-Map, and Optical-Depth) demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming existing detector-based and detector-free baselines in terms of accuracy and robustness. The implementation of the proposed method will be made publicly available at https://github.com/yeyuanxin110/VMGGA .

item.title: CSAP: Coarse-to-Fine Structured Angle Prediction for Oriented Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Beihang Song
Hongquan Sun
Tong Liu
Kai Zhu
Jun Wan
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Arbitrary-oriented object detection plays a crucial role in numerous vision applications. A key challenge lies in accurate orientation estimation, which suffers from angular periodicity and boundary discontinuity, leading to unstable learning and reduced accuracy. To address this issue, we propose a Coarse-to-Fine Structured Angle Prediction (CSAP) approach that reformulates angle estimation as a sequence of structured sub-tasks. Specifically, CSAP first discretizes continuous angles into coarse orientation intervals to ensure robust global prediction. Then, fine-grained angular components are modeled in a two-dimensional structured space, where elliptical Gaussian heatmaps capture local variations. An axis-rescaling strategy is further introduced to alleviate peak-shift errors and enhance localization precision. In addition, an optional multimodal contrastive alignment module is incorporated to provide semantic regularization by embedding category and orientation information into a shared representation space. This design provides a lightweight semantic regularization signal that can improve feature consistency and robustness to rotation variations. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that CSAP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and robustness. Published: 2026-05-21T19:44:36+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.822 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Beihang Song; Hongquan Sun; Tong Liu; Kai Zhu; Jun Wan

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3695690

Relevance: 0.822 (must_read)

Arbitrary-oriented object detection plays a crucial role in numerous vision applications. A key challenge lies in accurate orientation estimation, which suffers from angular periodicity and boundary discontinuity, leading to unstable learning and reduced accuracy. To address this issue, we propose a Coarse-to-Fine Structured Angle Prediction (CSAP) approach that reformulates angle estimation as a sequence of structured sub-tasks. Specifically, CSAP first discretizes continuous angles into coarse orientation intervals to ensure robust global prediction. Then, fine-grained angular components are modeled in a two-dimensional structured space, where elliptical Gaussian heatmaps capture local variations. An axis-rescaling strategy is further introduced to alleviate peak-shift errors and enhance localization precision. In addition, an optional multimodal contrastive alignment module is incorporated to provide semantic regularization by embedding category and orientation information into a shared representation space. This design provides a lightweight semantic regularization signal that can improve feature consistency and robustness to rotation variations. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that CSAP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and robustness.

item.title: Pose Guided Unsupervised Domain Adaptation for Human Body Part Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Arindam Dutta
Rohit Lal
Yash Garg
Calvin-Khang Ta
Dripta S. Raychaudhuri
Amit K. Roy-Chowdhury
item.description: Existing algorithms for human body part segmentation have shown promising results on challenging datasets, primarily relying on end-to-end supervision. However, these algorithms exhibit severe performance drops in the face of domain shifts, leading to inaccurate segmentation masks. To tackle this issue, we introduce POSTURE: Pose Guided Unsupervised Domain Adaptation for Human Body Part Segmentation - an innovative pseudo-labelling approach designed to improve segmentation performance on the unlabeled target data. Distinct from conventional domain adaptive methods for general semantic segmentation, POSTURE stands out by considering the underlying structure of the human body and uses anatomical guidance from pose keypoints to drive the adaptation process. This strong inductive prior translates to impressive performance improvements, averaging 8% over existing state-of-the-art domain adaptive semantic segmentation methods across three benchmark datasets. Furthermore, the inherent flexibility of our proposed approach facilitates seamless extension to source-free settings (SF-POSTURE), effectively mitigating potential privacy and computational concerns, with negligible drop in performance. Published: 2026-05-20T19:54:32+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.820 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Arindam Dutta; Rohit Lal; Yash Garg; Calvin-Khang Ta; Dripta S. Raychaudhuri; Amit K. Roy-Chowdhury

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3689414

Relevance: 0.820 (must_read)

Existing algorithms for human body part segmentation have shown promising results on challenging datasets, primarily relying on end-to-end supervision. However, these algorithms exhibit severe performance drops in the face of domain shifts, leading to inaccurate segmentation masks. To tackle this issue, we introduce POSTURE: Pose Guided Unsupervised Domain Adaptation for Human Body Part Segmentation - an innovative pseudo-labelling approach designed to improve segmentation performance on the unlabeled target data. Distinct from conventional domain adaptive methods for general semantic segmentation, POSTURE stands out by considering the underlying structure of the human body and uses anatomical guidance from pose keypoints to drive the adaptation process. This strong inductive prior translates to impressive performance improvements, averaging 8% over existing state-of-the-art domain adaptive semantic segmentation methods across three benchmark datasets. Furthermore, the inherent flexibility of our proposed approach facilitates seamless extension to source-free settings (SF-POSTURE), effectively mitigating potential privacy and computational concerns, with negligible drop in performance.

item.title: Training-Free Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation with Context Pyramid Refinement
item.dc:creator: Jialei Chen
Qi Fan
Zhenzhen Quan
Dongyue Li
Xu Zheng
Hiroshi Murase
Daisuke Deguchi
item.description: Training-free open-vocabulary semantic segmentation aims to adapt vision-language models, such as CLIP, for segmenting novel classes without any fine-tuning. However, existing methods often suffer from fragmented segmentation due to limited contextual awareness and weak object coherence. Subsequently, semantically related regions and different parts of the same object are often misclassified to different classes. To address these challenges, we enforce intra-object coherence and hierarchically refine contextual features across CLIP’s layers without any training. First, we introduce a context aggregation module that enhances both intra- and inter-layer contexts in CLIP features, thereby promoting consistent labeling of semantically related regions. Next, we propose an object-aware attention calibration module that integrates features from different regions of the same object to mitigate fragmentation. Finally, we inject object-level semantics into object features to ensure consistent category assignment across all parts. Our method refines CLIP features by leveraging hierarchical context and enforcing object-level coherence, leading to improved open-vocabulary segmentation performance without fine-tuning. Moreover, its plug-and-play design enables seamless integration into existing frameworks. Extensive experiments on eight benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Published: 2026-05-21T03:52:41+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.818 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jialei Chen; Qi Fan; Zhenzhen Quan; Dongyue Li; Xu Zheng; Hiroshi Murase; Daisuke Deguchi

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02813-3

Relevance: 0.818 (must_read)

Training-free open-vocabulary semantic segmentation aims to adapt vision-language models, such as CLIP, for segmenting novel classes without any fine-tuning. However, existing methods often suffer from fragmented segmentation due to limited contextual awareness and weak object coherence. Subsequently, semantically related regions and different parts of the same object are often misclassified to different classes. To address these challenges, we enforce intra-object coherence and hierarchically refine contextual features across CLIP’s layers without any training. First, we introduce a context aggregation module that enhances both intra- and inter-layer contexts in CLIP features, thereby promoting consistent labeling of semantically related regions. Next, we propose an object-aware attention calibration module that integrates features from different regions of the same object to mitigate fragmentation. Finally, we inject object-level semantics into object features to ensure consistent category assignment across all parts. Our method refines CLIP features by leveraging hierarchical context and enforcing object-level coherence, leading to improved open-vocabulary segmentation performance without fine-tuning. Moreover, its plug-and-play design enables seamless integration into existing frameworks. Extensive experiments on eight benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach.

item.title: Semantics Disentanglement and Composition for Universal Image Coding with Efficiently LLM Reasoning and Generative Diffusion
item.dc:creator: Jinming Liu
Yuntao Wei
Junyan Lin
Heming Sun
Shengyang Zhao
Zhibo Chen
Wenjun Zeng
Xin Jin
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Learned image compression methods have shown impressive performance but are often highly specialized for either human perception or specific machine vision tasks. This specialization limits their versatility and requires costly retraining for new applications. To address this, we introduce UniCodec, a universal codec built on a novel paradigm of semantic disentanglement at the encoder and compositional generation at the decoder. This framework is designed to simultaneously serve both human and machine needs, eliminating the need for task-specific retraining. At the encoder, UniCodec leverages pre-generated, task-specific label codebooks created by a Large Language Model (LLM). For any given task, a grounding model uses the corresponding codebook to perform task-aware disentanglement, compressing only the most relevant image regions. This mechanism not only saves significant bits but is also the key to our system’s rapid, zero-retraining adaptation: switching to a new task is as simple as selecting a new codebook. The decoder then performs compositional generation: it combines the compact, disentangled components with powerful priors from a generative diffusion model. This process reconstructs a high-quality, complete image optimized with rich detail for human perception and precise features for machine vision tasks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UniCodec consistently outperforms existing methods, effectively bridging the gap between human-centric and machine-centric compression. Published: 2026-05-20T19:54:18+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.816 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jinming Liu; Yuntao Wei; Junyan Lin; Heming Sun; Shengyang Zhao; Zhibo Chen; Wenjun Zeng; Xin Jin

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3695062

Relevance: 0.816 (must_read)

Learned image compression methods have shown impressive performance but are often highly specialized for either human perception or specific machine vision tasks. This specialization limits their versatility and requires costly retraining for new applications. To address this, we introduce UniCodec, a universal codec built on a novel paradigm of semantic disentanglement at the encoder and compositional generation at the decoder. This framework is designed to simultaneously serve both human and machine needs, eliminating the need for task-specific retraining. At the encoder, UniCodec leverages pre-generated, task-specific label codebooks created by a Large Language Model (LLM). For any given task, a grounding model uses the corresponding codebook to perform task-aware disentanglement, compressing only the most relevant image regions. This mechanism not only saves significant bits but is also the key to our system’s rapid, zero-retraining adaptation: switching to a new task is as simple as selecting a new codebook. The decoder then performs compositional generation: it combines the compact, disentangled components with powerful priors from a generative diffusion model. This process reconstructs a high-quality, complete image optimized with rich detail for human perception and precise features for machine vision tasks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UniCodec consistently outperforms existing methods, effectively bridging the gap between human-centric and machine-centric compression.

item.title: TerraGen: A Unified Multi-Task Layout Generation Framework for Remote Sensing Data Augmentation
item.dc:creator: Datao Tang
Hao Wang
Yudeng Xin
Hui Qiao
Dongsheng Jiang
Yin Li
Zhiheng Yu
Xiangyong Cao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Remote sensing vision tasks require extensive labeled data across multiple, interconnected domains. However, current generative data augmentation frameworks are task-isolated, i.e., each vision task requires training an independent generative model, and ignore the modeling of geographical information and spatial constraints. To address these issues, we propose TerraGen, a unified layout-to-image generation framework that enables flexible, spatially controllable synthesis of remote sensing imagery for various high-level vision tasks, e.g., detection, segmentation, and extraction. Specifically, Terra-Gen introduces a geographic-spatial layout encoder that unifies bounding box and segmentation mask inputs, combined with a multi-scale injection scheme and mask-weighted loss to explicitly encode spatial constraints, from global structures to fine details. Moreover, we construct the first large-scale multi-task remote sensing layout generation dataset and establish a standardized evaluation protocol for this task. Experimental results show that TerraGen achieves the best image generation quality across diverse tasks. Additionally, TerraGen can be used as a universal data-augmentation generator, enhancing downstream task performance significantly and demonstrating robust cross-task generalization in both full-data and few-shot scenarios. Published: 2026-05-20T19:52:37+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.810 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Datao Tang; Hao Wang; Yudeng Xin; Hui Qiao; Dongsheng Jiang; Yin Li; Zhiheng Yu; Xiangyong Cao

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3695344

Relevance: 0.810 (must_read)

Remote sensing vision tasks require extensive labeled data across multiple, interconnected domains. However, current generative data augmentation frameworks are task-isolated, i.e., each vision task requires training an independent generative model, and ignore the modeling of geographical information and spatial constraints. To address these issues, we propose TerraGen, a unified layout-to-image generation framework that enables flexible, spatially controllable synthesis of remote sensing imagery for various high-level vision tasks, e.g., detection, segmentation, and extraction. Specifically, Terra-Gen introduces a geographic-spatial layout encoder that unifies bounding box and segmentation mask inputs, combined with a multi-scale injection scheme and mask-weighted loss to explicitly encode spatial constraints, from global structures to fine details. Moreover, we construct the first large-scale multi-task remote sensing layout generation dataset and establish a standardized evaluation protocol for this task. Experimental results show that TerraGen achieves the best image generation quality across diverse tasks. Additionally, TerraGen can be used as a universal data-augmentation generator, enhancing downstream task performance significantly and demonstrating robust cross-task generalization in both full-data and few-shot scenarios.

item.title: Overlapping pyramid vision Transformer enables fine-grained SAR ship detection and classification
item.dc:creator: Zequn Sun
Jiajie Ma
Chunning Meng
Lijun Zhao
Lianzhi Huo
Tao Li
Zhiqing Zhang
Shengjiang Chang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Ship detection from synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images has gained increasing attention recently due to the irreplaceable all-weather imaging capabilities of SAR. However, it remains unclear whether SAR imaging in combination with modern deep-learning is capable of detecting and classifying ships at the instance level, which is hampered by the lack of datasets with multi-class fine-grained annotations and the accompanying ship detection methods. To tackle these issues, we build a new multi-class fine-grained SAR ship dataset ISSC and an efficient multi-scale model for fine-grained SAR ship detection and classification. The ISSC dataset has a number of 2764 SAR images and 8466 ship instances, with five categories. With this high-quality dataset that can reflect the diversity of ship scales, we further propose an efficient network for fine-grained SAR ship detection and classification (the ISSC model), which uses overlapping pyramid vision Transformer as the model backbone to extract multi-scale feature maps that prioritize the continuous global information. Subsequently, based on convolution units, two neck structures are designed to refine the directional features representing various multi-class ships and amplify the local semantic information effect of the feature cascading process, respectively. As such, the ISSC model is a novel global-to-local and direction-aware ship detection network for SAR images. Experiments on the ISSC dataset and the single-class ship dataset HRSID and SSDD demonstrate the superior performance of the ISSC model, which has a lower parameter count and achieves substantial improvements, compared to state-of-the-art models. Overall, we have demonstrated the feasibility of combining SAR imaging with the modern deep learning approach for fine-grained ship classification. Published: 2026-05-20T19:53:14+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.810 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zequn Sun; Jiajie Ma; Chunning Meng; Lijun Zhao; Lianzhi Huo; Tao Li; Zhiqing Zhang; Shengjiang Chang

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3695474

Relevance: 0.810 (must_read)

Ship detection from synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images has gained increasing attention recently due to the irreplaceable all-weather imaging capabilities of SAR. However, it remains unclear whether SAR imaging in combination with modern deep-learning is capable of detecting and classifying ships at the instance level, which is hampered by the lack of datasets with multi-class fine-grained annotations and the accompanying ship detection methods. To tackle these issues, we build a new multi-class fine-grained SAR ship dataset ISSC and an efficient multi-scale model for fine-grained SAR ship detection and classification. The ISSC dataset has a number of 2764 SAR images and 8466 ship instances, with five categories. With this high-quality dataset that can reflect the diversity of ship scales, we further propose an efficient network for fine-grained SAR ship detection and classification (the ISSC model), which uses overlapping pyramid vision Transformer as the model backbone to extract multi-scale feature maps that prioritize the continuous global information. Subsequently, based on convolution units, two neck structures are designed to refine the directional features representing various multi-class ships and amplify the local semantic information effect of the feature cascading process, respectively. As such, the ISSC model is a novel global-to-local and direction-aware ship detection network for SAR images. Experiments on the ISSC dataset and the single-class ship dataset HRSID and SSDD demonstrate the superior performance of the ISSC model, which has a lower parameter count and achieves substantial improvements, compared to state-of-the-art models. Overall, we have demonstrated the feasibility of combining SAR imaging with the modern deep learning approach for fine-grained ship classification.

item.title: TinySAM 2: Extreme Memory Compression for Efficient Track Anything Model
item.dc:creator: Zhaoyuan Ding
Yijing Yang
Han Shu
Xinghao Chen
item.description: Segment Anything Model 2 (SAM 2) serves as a core foundation model in the field of video segmentation. Building upon the original SAM model, it introduces a memory bank mechanism and demonstrates outstanding performance in tasks such as semi-supervised video object segmentation and tracking anything. However, the complex computational characteristics of SAM 2's multi-stage image encoder and memory module have raised the barrier to the model's deployment in practical applications. To address this issue, we propose TinySAM 2, a lightweight video segmentation model that balances performance and efficiency. First, a memory quality management mechanism is introduced to select and retain high-informative historical frames as the memory. In addition, a joint-spatial-temporal token compression is proposed that reduces the memory storage and computational cost. Specifically, average pooling is employed to first compress redundancy tokens in the spatial domain. In the temporal domain, informative tokens are selected across frames in the memory bank based on token-level similarity measurement. Besides, we take RepViT as the lightweight image encoder, which further reduces the model parameters. Extensive experiments on challenging datasets such as DAVIS and SA-V demonstrate that TinySAM 2 achieves 90% of the performance of SAM 2.1, with only 7% memory tokens and 3% training data. This study effectively alleviates the bottlenecks in parameter count, computational load, and deployment costs associated with SAM 2, providing a resource-efficient solution for the widespread application of video segmentation models on devices. Published: 2026-05-18T08:05:59+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.808 (must_read)

Authors: Zhaoyuan Ding; Yijing Yang; Han Shu; Xinghao Chen

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.808 (must_read)

Segment Anything Model 2 (SAM 2) serves as a core foundation model in the field of video segmentation. Building upon the original SAM model, it introduces a memory bank mechanism and demonstrates outstanding performance in tasks such as semi-supervised video object segmentation and tracking anything. However, the complex computational characteristics of SAM 2's multi-stage image encoder and memory module have raised the barrier to the model's deployment in practical applications. To address this issue, we propose TinySAM 2, a lightweight video segmentation model that balances performance and efficiency. First, a memory quality management mechanism is introduced to select and retain high-informative historical frames as the memory. In addition, a joint-spatial-temporal token compression is proposed that reduces the memory storage and computational cost. Specifically, average pooling is employed to first compress redundancy tokens in the spatial domain. In the temporal domain, informative tokens are selected across frames in the memory bank based on token-level similarity measurement. Besides, we take RepViT as the lightweight image encoder, which further reduces the model parameters. Extensive experiments on challenging datasets such as DAVIS and SA-V demonstrate that TinySAM 2 achieves 90% of the performance of SAM 2.1, with only 7% memory tokens and 3% training data. This study effectively alleviates the bottlenecks in parameter count, computational load, and deployment costs associated with SAM 2, providing a resource-efficient solution for the widespread application of video segmentation models on devices.

item.title: Multi-modal prompt interaction for visual unsupervised domain adaptation
item.dc:creator: Yanan Li
Shiyu Quan
Feiyang Zhang
Hao Lu
Zhijiang Li
Junfeng Qu
item.description: Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) aims to develop a model capable of transferring knowledge from a well-labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain. Traditional UDA methods attempt to learn invariant features by aligning data distributions between source and target domains. These methods simply map labels to one-hot encodings, which fail to capture the intrinsic semantic correlations among categories. Large Vision-Language pre-trained Models (VLMs), such as Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP), consider both image and label information, offering new possibilities for UDA tasks. The UDA methods utilize CLIP predominantly concentrate on adjusting text prompts. They neglect certain visual context information that is lost during the encoding phase, which hinders knowledge transfer. To effectively utilize multi-modal information, a novel framework M ulti- m odal P rompts I nteraction ( M2PI ) is proposed. Specifically, the discarded pre-trained knowledge is aggregated into dual-branch learnable prompt tokens, where regularization is imposed on standard prompt learning. The two prompt branches interact via a prompt interaction module. This process is further regularized through an instance-discrimination contrastive loss and an information maximization loss. M2PI achieved the best average classification accuracy of 78.6%/87.6% with ResNet/ViT-B/16 and 63.8% with ViT-B/16 on the Office-Home and DomainNet datasets, and the second-best average classification accuracy of 88.1%/90.8% with ResNet/ViT-B/16 on the VisDA-2017 dataset. The results on three UDA benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority of M2PI over the state-of-the-art approaches. Published: 2026-05-20T23:27:28+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.808 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yanan Li; Shiyu Quan; Feiyang Zhang; Hao Lu; Zhijiang Li; Junfeng Qu

Journal: Neurocomputing

DOI: 10.1016/j.neucom.2026.133941

Relevance: 0.808 (must_read)

Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) aims to develop a model capable of transferring knowledge from a well-labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain. Traditional UDA methods attempt to learn invariant features by aligning data distributions between source and target domains. These methods simply map labels to one-hot encodings, which fail to capture the intrinsic semantic correlations among categories. Large Vision-Language pre-trained Models (VLMs), such as Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP), consider both image and label information, offering new possibilities for UDA tasks. The UDA methods utilize CLIP predominantly concentrate on adjusting text prompts. They neglect certain visual context information that is lost during the encoding phase, which hinders knowledge transfer. To effectively utilize multi-modal information, a novel framework M ulti- m odal P rompts I nteraction ( M2PI ) is proposed. Specifically, the discarded pre-trained knowledge is aggregated into dual-branch learnable prompt tokens, where regularization is imposed on standard prompt learning. The two prompt branches interact via a prompt interaction module. This process is further regularized through an instance-discrimination contrastive loss and an information maximization loss. M2PI achieved the best average classification accuracy of 78.6%/87.6% with ResNet/ViT-B/16 and 63.8% with ViT-B/16 on the Office-Home and DomainNet datasets, and the second-best average classification accuracy of 88.1%/90.8% with ResNet/ViT-B/16 on the VisDA-2017 dataset. The results on three UDA benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority of M2PI over the state-of-the-art approaches.

item.title: JointTrack: Densely joint feature extraction and interaction via temporal–spatial prompt learning for object tracking in multi-drone videos
item.dc:creator: Han Wu
Kui Liu
Yuli Sun
Hao Sun
Kefeng Ji
Gangyao Kuang
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Object tracking in multi-drone videos shows great potential in disaster emergency response, scene reconstruction and low-altitude economy due to its advantages of wide-area coverage and comprehensive perception. Most existing trackers adopt a decoupled framework, where each drone independently performs multi-stage feature extraction before multi-drone interaction. However, this paradigm suffers from inherent limitations, including task conflicts, mutual feature contamination and inconsistent target representations. To overcome above issues, this paper proposes JointTrack, a temporal–spatial prompt learning-based framework densely joints feature extraction and interaction in an end-to-end manner. Unlike prior methods, JointTrack incorporates multi-drone interaction at every feature extraction stage to achieve collaborative optimization. Specifically, a temporal prompt encoder (TPE) is proposed to capture temporal prompts from historical frames, guiding the network to disentangle target-related dynamics from background noise and thereby suppress feature contamination caused by occlusions or cluttered scenes. Meanwhile, a spatial prompt encoder (SPE) constructs scale-aware and component-aware prompts by integrating multi-drone templates, enabling feature complementarity that alleviate inconsistent feature representations under varying viewpoints. Additionally, a dynamic gated decoder (DGD) adaptively fuse these temporal–spatial prompts into original features through a gating mechanism with contextual dependency modeling. Extensive experiments on the MDOT benchmark demonstrate that JointTrack outperforms previous state-of-the-art trackers by 4.1% and 3.0% in success and precision. Moreover, JointTrack maintains competitive tracking speed and exhibits strong generalizability on the MDMT and UAV123 datasets, as well as in real-world scenarios. Codes will be released at https://github.com/HanJin0326/JointTrack . Published: 2026-05-21T17:23:17+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.805 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Han Wu; Kui Liu; Yuli Sun; Hao Sun; Kefeng Ji; Gangyao Kuang

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.05.016

Relevance: 0.805 (must_read)

Object tracking in multi-drone videos shows great potential in disaster emergency response, scene reconstruction and low-altitude economy due to its advantages of wide-area coverage and comprehensive perception. Most existing trackers adopt a decoupled framework, where each drone independently performs multi-stage feature extraction before multi-drone interaction. However, this paradigm suffers from inherent limitations, including task conflicts, mutual feature contamination and inconsistent target representations. To overcome above issues, this paper proposes JointTrack, a temporal–spatial prompt learning-based framework densely joints feature extraction and interaction in an end-to-end manner. Unlike prior methods, JointTrack incorporates multi-drone interaction at every feature extraction stage to achieve collaborative optimization. Specifically, a temporal prompt encoder (TPE) is proposed to capture temporal prompts from historical frames, guiding the network to disentangle target-related dynamics from background noise and thereby suppress feature contamination caused by occlusions or cluttered scenes. Meanwhile, a spatial prompt encoder (SPE) constructs scale-aware and component-aware prompts by integrating multi-drone templates, enabling feature complementarity that alleviate inconsistent feature representations under varying viewpoints. Additionally, a dynamic gated decoder (DGD) adaptively fuse these temporal–spatial prompts into original features through a gating mechanism with contextual dependency modeling. Extensive experiments on the MDOT benchmark demonstrate that JointTrack outperforms previous state-of-the-art trackers by 4.1% and 3.0% in success and precision. Moreover, JointTrack maintains competitive tracking speed and exhibits strong generalizability on the MDMT and UAV123 datasets, as well as in real-world scenarios. Codes will be released at https://github.com/HanJin0326/JointTrack .

item.title: Neural operators for free-boundary problems
item.dc:creator: Constantinos Siettos
item.description: Free boundary problems, such as modelling glacier melt, are difficult to capture with neural operators. A new framework addresses this challenge by leveraging the mathematical principle of topological conjugacy. item.title: SkyNative: A Native Multimodal Framework for Remote Sensing Visual Evidence Reasoning
item.dc:creator: Xiao Yang
Ronghao Fu
Zhiwen Lin
Zhuoran Duan
Jiashun Zhu
Jiasen Hu
Lang Sun
Weipeng Zhang
Jiaqi Liu
Xu Na
Haoran Liu
Weijie Zhang
Bo Yang
item.description: Remote sensing vision-language models commonly rely on pretrained visual encoders to convert images into semantic features before language-model reasoning. While effective for scene-level understanding, this pipeline may prematurely compress local visual evidence, making fine-grained spatial reasoning vulnerable to language priors, especially in ultra-high-resolution remote sensing imagery. We present SkyNative, a native multimodal framework for remote sensing that adopts an encoder-free architecture, removing the pretrained visual backbone to directly represent images as raw patch tokens in the language-model token space. To reconcile low-level visual patches with textual tokens, SkyNative introduces a modality-aware decoupling mechanism that uses modality-specific parameters within a unified autoregressive backbone. We further introduce a visual reliance benchmark that diagnoses whether models ground their answers in image evidence through progressive visual degradation and misleading textual prompts. Across standard remote sensing understanding tasks and large-format spatial reasoning evaluations, SkyNative shows stronger image-grounded perception and improved robustness against prompt-induced language priors. These results suggest that native patch-level multimodal modeling is a promising direction for reliable remote sensing vision-language reasoning. Published: 2026-05-18T07:06:23+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.804 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiao Yang; Ronghao Fu; Zhiwen Lin; Zhuoran Duan; Jiashun Zhu; Jiasen Hu; Lang Sun; Weipeng Zhang; Jiaqi Liu; Xu Na; Haoran Liu; Weijie Zhang; Bo Yang

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.804 (must_read)

Remote sensing vision-language models commonly rely on pretrained visual encoders to convert images into semantic features before language-model reasoning. While effective for scene-level understanding, this pipeline may prematurely compress local visual evidence, making fine-grained spatial reasoning vulnerable to language priors, especially in ultra-high-resolution remote sensing imagery. We present SkyNative, a native multimodal framework for remote sensing that adopts an encoder-free architecture, removing the pretrained visual backbone to directly represent images as raw patch tokens in the language-model token space. To reconcile low-level visual patches with textual tokens, SkyNative introduces a modality-aware decoupling mechanism that uses modality-specific parameters within a unified autoregressive backbone. We further introduce a visual reliance benchmark that diagnoses whether models ground their answers in image evidence through progressive visual degradation and misleading textual prompts. Across standard remote sensing understanding tasks and large-format spatial reasoning evaluations, SkyNative shows stronger image-grounded perception and improved robustness against prompt-induced language priors. These results suggest that native patch-level multimodal modeling is a promising direction for reliable remote sensing vision-language reasoning.

item.title: Efficient Composition of Modal Experts for Versatile Multimodal Large Language Models
item.dc:creator: Tongtian Yue
Longteng Guo
Xuange Gao
Zijia Zhao
Hua Huang
Jing Liu
item.description: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown promise in comprehending various specialized modalities, yet developing a versatile model proficient in multiple modalities remains challenging and resource-intensive. In this paper, we explore efficient methods for training MLLMs to master various modalities. We first introduce a novel framework, the Multimodal Experts Composition (MixLLM), which enables the efficient and scalable integration of modality-specific MLLMs into a unified comprehensive model. Specifically, MixLLM adopts a modular Mixture of Experts (MoE) design with both modality-specific experts and shared experts. We propose a hybrid routing mechanism that adaptively coordinates the use of these two types of experts, enabling each token to benefit from both specialized modality knowledge and shared semantic representations. This design enables strong unimodal performance and flexible cross-modal reasoning. To further enhance the model's capability in following cross-modal instructions, we then propose the Multimodal Samples Composition (MixSample), a simple yet effective strategy for cross-modal instruction tuning. MixSample innovatively combines samples from different modalities into pseudo cross-modal datasets, thereby reducing the dependency on scarce and complex-to-acquire fully paired multimodal data. Implementing both MixLLM and MixSample, we develop MixtralX-11×7B , a model adept at handling inputs from various combinations of text, image, video, and audio modalities. Featured with a sparsely-activated MoE architecture, the model scales up parameters efficiently while maintaining computational costs. Extensive evaluations across various unimodal and cross-modal benchmarks demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms existing methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance across all evaluated tasks with a single unified model. Both our model and code will be publicly available. Published: 2026-05-21T19:43:37+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia Score: 0.802 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tongtian Yue; Longteng Guo; Xuange Gao; Zijia Zhao; Hua Huang; Jing Liu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia

DOI: 10.1109/tmm.2026.3695784

Relevance: 0.802 (must_read)

Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown promise in comprehending various specialized modalities, yet developing a versatile model proficient in multiple modalities remains challenging and resource-intensive. In this paper, we explore efficient methods for training MLLMs to master various modalities. We first introduce a novel framework, the Multimodal Experts Composition (MixLLM), which enables the efficient and scalable integration of modality-specific MLLMs into a unified comprehensive model. Specifically, MixLLM adopts a modular Mixture of Experts (MoE) design with both modality-specific experts and shared experts. We propose a hybrid routing mechanism that adaptively coordinates the use of these two types of experts, enabling each token to benefit from both specialized modality knowledge and shared semantic representations. This design enables strong unimodal performance and flexible cross-modal reasoning. To further enhance the model's capability in following cross-modal instructions, we then propose the Multimodal Samples Composition (MixSample), a simple yet effective strategy for cross-modal instruction tuning. MixSample innovatively combines samples from different modalities into pseudo cross-modal datasets, thereby reducing the dependency on scarce and complex-to-acquire fully paired multimodal data. Implementing both MixLLM and MixSample, we develop MixtralX-11×7B , a model adept at handling inputs from various combinations of text, image, video, and audio modalities. Featured with a sparsely-activated MoE architecture, the model scales up parameters efficiently while maintaining computational costs. Extensive evaluations across various unimodal and cross-modal benchmarks demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms existing methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance across all evaluated tasks with a single unified model. Both our model and code will be publicly available.

item.title: TextTeacher: What Can Language Teach About Images?
item.dc:creator: Tobias Christian Nauen
Stanislav Frolov
Brian Bernhard Moser
Federico Raue
Ahmed Anwar
Andreas Dengel
item.description: The platonic representation hypothesis suggests that sufficiently large models converge to a shared representation geometry, even across modalities. Motivated by this, we ask: Can the semantic knowledge of a language model efficiently improve a vision model? As an answer, we introduce TextTeacher, a simple auxiliary objective that injects text embeddings as additional information into image classification training. TextTeacher uses readily available image captions, a pre-trained and frozen text encoder, and a lightweight projection to produce semantic anchors that efficiently guide representations during training while leaving the inference-time model unchanged. On ImageNet with standard ViT backbones, TextTeacher improves accuracy by up to +2.7 percentage points (p.p.) and yields consistent transfer gains (on average +1.0 p.p.) under the same recipe and compute. It outperforms vision knowledge distillation, yielding more accuracy at a constant compute budget or similar accuracy, but 33% faster. Our analysis indicates that TextTeacher acts as a feature-space preconditioner, shaping deeper layers in the first stages of training, and aiding generalization by supplying complementary semantic cues. TextTeacher adds negligible overhead, requires no costly multimodal training of the target model and preserves the simplicity and latency of pure vision models. Project page with code and captions: https://nauen-it.de/publications/text-teacher Published: 2026-05-21T07:36:00+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.801 (must_read)
